entries.title: Hegseth says Iran war has cost $37.5 billion so far
entries.summary: US Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth is also pushing for more funding for the war. It comes as the US military is striking Iran for the 11th consecutive night. DW has the latest.
entries.content:
United States of America

Hegseth says Iran war has cost $37.5 billion so far

Published July 22, 2026last updated July 22, 2026

US Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth is also pushing for more funding for the war. It comes as the US military is striking Iran for the 11th consecutive night. DW has the latest.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth requests fresh funding for Iran War in remarks to senators
  • Republicans unveil $95 billion military funding plan
  • Hegseth's testimony was interrrupted by protesters against the Iran war
  • US strikes Iran for 11th consecutive night, CENTCOM says
  • Explosions reported in Tehran for first time since US-Iran ceasefire fell apart on July 8
  • Trump allows direct US flights to Lebanon in historic decision

Follow below for the latest developments on the US war with Iran and news from the wider Middle East:

Skip next section US completes 11th straight night of strikes on Iran

US completes 11th straight night of strikes on Iran

US Central Command (CENTCOM), says its latest operation targeted Iranian military operations centers, maritime assets, aircraft hangars, drone storage sites and logistics infrastructure.

CENTCOM accused Iran of attacking more than 30 commercial vessels in the strategic waterway over the past three months, saying the attacks endangered civilian crews and disrupted freedom of navigation.

Despite the ongoing conflict, the US military said the Strait of Hormuz remains open to commercial traffic. Since early May, CENTCOM says it has helped facilitate the transit of around 900 commercial vessels carrying approximately 450 million barrels of crude oil through the region.

Skip next section READ: Trump allows direct US flights to Lebanon in historic move

READ: Trump allows direct US flights to Lebanon in historic move

The US will allow direct flights to Lebanon, President Donald Trump announced.

It comes over 40 years after the US suspended direct flights between the two countries in 1985, during US President Ronald Reagan's administration, in the wake of the hijacking of TWA Flight 847.

Skip next section Explosions reported in Tehran for first time since latest escalation with US

Explosions reported in Tehran for first time since latest escalation with US

Iran activated air defense systems over Tehran early Wednesday, with explosions heard in the northeastern part of the capital, according to Iranian state media.

State television reported air defense activity in several districts across Tehran, including the western, eastern and northeastern parts of the city, which is home to around 9 million people.

A reporter with German news agency DPA also reported explosions in the Iranian capital.

The reports mark the first explosions in Tehran since the latest escalation in the conflict between Iran and the United States. A 60-day ceasefire between the two sides fell apart on July 8.

Skip next section US launches 11th consecutive night of strikes on Iran

US launches 11th consecutive night of strikes on Iran

The US military says it has begun an 11th consecutive night of strikes against targets in Iran.

US Central Command, or CENTCOM, said the operation started at 7 p.m. Eastern Time (11 p.m. UTC/GMT) and is aimed at further reducing Iran's ability to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

The latest attacks are part of an ongoing US campaign targeting Iranian military capabilities linked to maritime operations and regional security.

Iranian media reported fresh explosions around Sirik in southern Iran.

Skip next section Pentagon seeks billions more for Iran war

Pentagon seeks billions more for Iran war

US Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth faced tough questioning from lawmakers on Tuesday as he defended the growing cost of the US military campaign against Iran.

Speaking before the Senate Appropriations Committee, Hegseth said the conflict has so far cost $37.5 billion (€32.89 billion), a figure significantly higher than earlier estimates. He argued that additional funding is urgently needed, warning that the military faces critical shortfalls without it.

The hearing was repeatedly interrupted by protesters and came days after the Pentagon confirmed that three more US service members had died in the conflict, bringing the American death toll to 17.

Democratic senators challenged both the cost and direction of the war. Senator Patty Murray warned of "another forever war," while other lawmakers questioned why the Pentagon was seeking more funding when some previously approved military spending has yet to be used.

The debate comes as Republicans push a $95 billion budget package that includes tens of billions for the military and national security, as well as funding for agriculture and election-related measures.

Meanwhile, opinion polls suggest many Americans remain critical of US President Donald Trump's handling of the conflict as fighting with Iran continues.

Skip next section Welcome to our coverage

Welcome to our coverage

Welcome to our coverage of the US war with Iran.

US Pentagon chief Pete Hegseth spoke before the Senate Appropriations Committee and asked for urgent additional funds for the Iran war. The conflict so far has cost the US an estimated $37.5 billion (€32.89 billion).

It comes as the US military says it is striking Iran for the 11th consecutive night.

Stay tuned as we bring you the latest on the US conflict with Iran and other developments from the Middle East.

entries.title: Trump allows direct US flights to Lebanon in historic move
entries.summary: It comes over 40 years after the US suspended the direct route with Lebanon. The announcement was made after US President Donald Trump met with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun.
entries.content:
Middle East

Trump allows direct US flights to Lebanon in historic move

July 22, 2026

It comes over 40 years after the US suspended the direct route with Lebanon. The announcement was made after US President Donald Trump met with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun.

The US will allow direct flights to Lebanon, President Donald Trump announced on Tuesday. 

It comes over 40 years after the US suspended direct flights between the two countries in 1985, during US President Ronald Reagan's administration, in the wake of the hijacking of TWA Flight 847.

"I am hereby directing my Administration to allow all US airline carriers to fly directly to Lebanon so that Americans can easily visit this beautiful land," Trump said in a post on his Truth Social platform.

Lebanese Prime Minister Nawaf Salam hailed Trump's announcement. Salam said it "represents a significant opportunity to enhance travel and connectivity between Lebanon and the United States, and to support the Lebanese economy."

What did Trump say about the resumption of direct flights between the US and Lebanon?

The announcement comes after Trump hosted Lebanese President Michel Aoun at the White House on Tuesday.

During that meeting, Trump told Aoun that Lebanon had "been a very badly treated place and country and we’re going to have it properly treated and treated with the respect it deserves."

"We’re gonna help it a lot," he said, without providing specific details.

Lebanon appeals to US, Trump to help secure lasting peace

Washington has been mediating rare talks between Israel and Lebanon, but has become distracted in recent weeks by the resumption of full-scale hostilities between the US and Iran.

Aoun, who was making the first visit to the US capital by a Lebanese president since 2009, had traveled to Washington, D.C. to appeal for a longer-term, more stable peace in his country, which has been plagued by months of war between Israel and the Iran-backed, Lebanon-based militant group Hezbollah.

Aside from the resumption of direct flights between the US and Lebanon, authorities in Beirut will be hoping that this week's talks in Washington yield an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon, where Israel's ground forces have occupied swathes of territory as part of its campaign against Hezbollah.

Trump claimed Israel's military was "in the process of doing that. They’re in the process of redeploying," he said, without elaborating.

Israel is occupying southern Lebanon in what it says is an effort to protect northern Israeli communities from Hezbollah attacks. Israel, along with the US, Germany and some other countries, recognize Hezbollah as a terror group.

Aoun hails Trump as 'great president'

Aoun, for his part, heaped praise on Trump, whom he called a "great president" whose "vision is peace."

"I do agree with that," Trump jokingly replied, calling Aoun handsome and someone who "knows how to get to me."

"Now he can have anything," Trump added.

Edited by: Wesley Dockery

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Spain: Firefighters battle major wildfire north of Madrid
entries.summary: Officials say the blaze, which has forced about 1,200 people to evacuate and has burned at least 30,000 hectares of land, is one of the most complex to affect the country in years.
entries.content:
Spain

Spain: Firefighters battle major wildfire north of Madrid

July 21, 2026

Officials say the blaze, which has forced about 1,200 people to evacuate and has burned at least 30,000 hectares of land, is one of the most complex to affect the country in years.

Hundreds of firefighters and soldiers were battling a massive wildfire northeast of Madrid on Tuesday that has been raging since last week.

Authorities say the blaze, which broke out on Thursday in Guadalajara province about 100 kilometers (60 miles) north of Spain's capital, is one of the most difficult they have faced and is considered the most significant wildfire to affect the country this year.

What do we know about the fire north of Madrid?

"It is the most complex of those that have been tackled in recent years in Spain," Castilla-La Mancha regional president Emiliano Garcia-Page told reporters.

Garcia-Page added that more than 600 firefighters and nearly 50 aircraft and helicopters had "practically stopped" the blaze from spreading toward the provincial capital of Soria, but that it was not yet under control.

"We can now see light at the end of the tunnel," he said.

The blaze has forced some 1,200 people to evacuate from their homes across the region of Castilla-La Mancha, while at least 30,000 hectares (74,000 acres) of land have been burnt, officials said.

The fire has been so difficult to bring under control because the area is made up of scrubland and has been under prolonged, severe drought conditions. High winds have also complicated the situation.

Spain's risk of more fires 'remains high'

Spain on Tuesday entered the third heatwave of the summer so far, with temperatures expected to soar above 40 degrees Celsius (104 Fahrenheit) in much of the country, hindering firefighting efforts.

According to Spain's national weather agency AEMET, the heatwave is expected to only last about three days.

Spain is still reeling from one of the deadliest wildfires in modern times that killed 13 people and destroyed 7,000 hectares in southern Almeria province earlier this month.

According to the European Forest Fire Information System (EFFIS), wildfires have destroyed over 115,000 hectares of land in Spain this year.

"We are going through particularly complex weeks, and the risk of fires remains extremely high," government spokeswoman Elma Saiz told a press conference.

Last year, the EFFIS said more than 393,000 hectares were destroyed by wildfires, the most in Spain's history.

France also battling major fires

In southwestern France, two firefighters were killed near an airport in Gironde, Interior Minister Laurent Nunez said Tuesday.

On Monday, firefighters tackled a fire at a Roma camp on the outskirts of Nantes in western France.

Around 200 people were evacuated after the blaze, which began after a car caught fire, spread to nearby woodland and to caravans belonging to people living in the settlement.

Human-driven global heating is making heatwaves longer, more intense and more frequent, most scientists say, which creates dry conditions that increase the likelihood of destructive wildfires.

Edited by: Wesley Dockery

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Ukraine's Zelenskyy fires military chief after days of protests
entries.summary: Mass protests in recent days in Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities had called for the removal of commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi. Volodymyr Zelenskyy has named Mykhailo Drapatyi as the army's new chief.
entries.content:
Ukraine

Ukraine: Zelenskyy replaces army chief after protests

July 21, 2026

Mass protests in recent days in Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities had called for the removal of commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi. Volodymyr Zelenskyy has named Mykhailo Drapatyi as the army's new chief.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy announced on Tuesday that he was removing Oleksandr Syrskyi, the army's commander-in-chief, from his post.

"I have decided that the new Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces of Ukraine will be Mykhailo Drapatyi... I am grateful to Oleksandr Syrskyi and to each of our warriors for Ukraine's strong front-line positions," Zelenskyy said in a post on Telegram.

"Our shared wish is one — victory over the enemy and conditions at the front and in pressure on Russia that would force Russia toward peace," Zelenskyy said. "A significant path has been traveled, the defense of Ukraine continues, and every warrior deserves dignified treatment."

Why did Zelenskyy sack his commander-in-chief?

The move comes as Zelenskyy has been facing mounting criticism over the dismissal of Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov last week.

Following his sacking, Fedorov, 35, went public with his conflict with Syrskyi. Their dispute was mainly over a disagreement about the direction of Ukraine's war effort against Russia's ongoing invasion.

Fedorov had accused Syrskyi of blocking his attempts at reforming and modernizing the army.

Fedorov's removal triggered massive, nationwide protests that saw several thousand, mostly young people demonstrate in Ukraine's capital, Kyiv and other cities. They demanded that Syrskyi be removed and that Fedorov be reinstated.

Zelenskyy, who had initially sided with Syrskyi, eventually bowed to public pressure on Tuesday, after days of meetings with dozens of Ukraine's top military figures.

Who is sacked Ukrainian general Oleksandr Syrskyi?

Syrskyi, 60, was born in Russia and was trained in Moscow during the Soviet era.

He had led some of Ukraine's most decisive campaigns, including halting Russia's initial advance towards Kyiv in 2022 and leading Ukraine's incursion into Kursk, Russia in 2024.

"It is a fact that Oleksandr Syrskyi ensured Ukraine's successes in the defense of Kyiv, in the Kharkiv offensive operation, and in the Kursk operation," Ukraine's president said on Tuesday when announcing the change.

But the military veteran had clashed with reformers over a range of issues, including the use of drones and technology.

His dismissal caps a dramatic week in Ukrainian politics, which has seen Zelenskyy botch a leadership reshuffle that has triggered some of the largest protests the country has seen since Russia's full-scale invasion began in 2022.

How has ex-Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov reacted?

Fedorov, a reformist, was associated with Ukraine's drone strikes against targets in Russia, which have helped turn the tide of the war and garnered wide praise in the West.

Fedorov said Syrskyi's replacement as Ukraine's commander-in-chief by Drapatyi brings "fresh hope" to the country.

"This is a breath of fresh air and a new hope in the struggle of free people for freedom and justice," Fedorov said on social media.

Edited by: Wesley Dockery

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking hereand hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Trump says the United States is 'not finished' with Iran
entries.summary: A 10th straight day of hostilities between the United States and Iran across the Middle East overshadowed diplomatic efforts in Pakistan to salvage an interim ceasefire deal.
entries.content:
Iran

Trump says the United States is 'not finished' with Iran

Published July 20, 2026last updated July 21, 2026

A 10th straight day of hostilities between the United States and Iran across the Middle East overshadowed diplomatic efforts in Pakistan to salvage an interim ceasefire deal.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • Trump says US military campaign against Iran will continue
  • Iran struck sites in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan
  • Trump says US will be hitting Pickaxe Mountain area 'pretty soon'

  • Two tankers carrying Saudi crude make U-turns in Red Sea after Houthi warning

  • Saudi Arabia condemns Houthis' Red Sea blockade

This blog is now closed. For the latest on Tuesday, please read here.

Below is a roundup of the main developments from the US-Iran war on July 21, 2026.

Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click here to add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.

Skip next section Trump warns US will respond to any Houthi blockade of Saudi ports

Trump warns US will respond to any Houthi blockade of Saudi ports

President Donald Trump says the US will respond if Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthi movement carries out its threat to impose a naval blockade on Saudi ports.

Trump told reporters that if the Houthis take action, "we will just have to take care of business."

"You know, we've done that with the Houthis before, and we haven't heard from them in a while since we did what we did originally. "

The Houthis, who control much of northern and western Yemen, including the Red Sea coast, announced the blockade on Monday, opening a potential new front in the wider regional conflict involving Iran, Israel and the United States.

The announcement follows renewed clashes between Saudi Arabia and the Houthis, raising concerns about the future of a truce that has largely held since 2022.

Two tankers carrying Saudi crude to China and India reportedly changed course on Tuesday, avoiding the Bab el-Mandeb Strait connect the Red Sea to the Gulf of Aden.

Skip next section Trump says US not finished with Iran campaign, dismisses immediate talks

Trump says US not finished with Iran campaign, dismisses immediate talks

President Donald Trump said the United States will continue its military campaign against Iran.

Speaking during a White House meeting with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun, Trump said Iran would need 20 to 25 years to rebuild from the damage already sustained.

"If we left right now, it would take Iran 20, 25 years to rebuild. Okay, and we're not finished at all ... we're not leaving right now," he told reporters.

Trump also expressed skepticism about potential talks aimed at ending the conflict.

"I will tell you, they want to desperately meet. And until they're ready to meet in a meaningful way, we have no interest in meeting," Trump told reporters.

He said that in dialogue behind the scenes, Iranian officials had said "they want to meet desperately to try and end it, because they're getting decimated."

Trump also said the US would be ‌hitting ⁠the ⁠Pickaxe Mountain area, located ​near Iran's heavily damaged ​Natanz uranium ​enrichment ‌facility, "pretty soon."

Skip next section Lebanon's Aoun urges Trump to back Lebanese military

Lebanon's Aoun urges Trump to back Lebanese military

Lebanese President Joseph Aoun has urged US President Donald Trump to maintain support for Lebanon's military, warning that southern Lebanon could slide back into the kind of instability seen in the 1970s without continued backing.

"We don't want to see that back again. All we're asking is, keep supporting the Lebanese Armed Forces," Aoun told reporters.

Speaking at a meeting with US President Donald Trump at the White House, Aoun said he fully trusts the Lebanese Armed Forces and called for ongoing US assistance to help secure the country's south.

Trump vowed the US would help Lebanon going forward.

"It's been a very badly treated place and country, and we're going to have it properly treated, and treated with the respect that it deserves," Trump said.

The talks come as Lebanon seeks the withdrawal of Israeli troops from parts of southern Lebanon.

Lebanon and Israel announced a framework agreement on June 26.

The US-mediated deal is aimed at ending the fighting between Israel and Hezbollah, an Iran-backed Shiite militant group in Lebanon.

Lebanon is seeking greater support for its military to take control of former Hezbollah strongholds.

Skip next section France summons Iranian charge d'affaires over Tehran embassy incident

France summons Iranian charge d'affaires over Tehran embassy incident

France's Foreign Ministry summoned the Iranian ‌charge ⁠d'affaires ⁠on Tuesday over an incident where it said French Embassy staff in Tehran had been physically intimidated by Iranian security services.  

A spokesperson said the ministry's secretary-general had "informed him of our strongest condemnation of this premeditated and deliberate aggression."

"France ​expects the Iranian authorities to shed ​light on this incident, punish the ⁠perpetrators, ​and ​ensure the security of ​its premises ‌and personnel, in ​accordance ⁠with their international obligations," ⁠the ​spokesperson said.

When reporting the incident two days ago, French Foreign Minister Jean-Noel Barrot said the staff members had been treated in a manner that was "in flagrant violation of diplomatic immunity."

Both were "detained for several hours without reason, interrogated, and one of them was physically abused" before they were able to return to the embassy, he said.

Skip next section WATCH: Iran war: Analyzing the Houthi blockade against Saudi Arabia

WATCH: Iran war: Analyzing the Houthi blockade against Saudi Arabia

As the Iran war ripples across the Middle East, Yemen's Houthis threaten a maritime blockade against Saudi Arabia in the strategic Bab al-Mandeb Strait. What risk does this blockade pose to regional security

Skip next section Kuwait says Iran hit several power, water plants on Monday strikes

Kuwait says Iran hit several power, water plants on Monday strikes

Iran struck several power generation and water desalination plants in attacks on Monday, according to Kuwait's Ministry of Electricity, Water and Renewable Energy.

"In light of the continued Iranian heinous aggressions on the state of Kuwait, several electricity power plants and water desalination stations were subjected to attacks for the fourth day in a row resulting in fires in several facilities," the ministry said in a post on X.

The ministry said a number of power generation units were taken out of service to ensure the stability of the electricity and water grid.

Authorities have called on the public to ration their electricity use due to the attacks, which come amid the scorching summer heat in the Gulf country, where residents survive off of constant air conditioning.

Skip next section Iranian minister meeting with mediators in Pakistan — reports

Iranian minister meeting with mediators in Pakistan — reports

News agencies are reporting that Iran's Interior Minister Eskandar Momeni is in Pakistan to meet with mediators amid continued US and Iranian strikes.

The Associated Press reported that Momeni had met Pakistan’s army chief Field Marshal Asim Munir to discuss the closed-door negotiations and cited two officials who spoke on condition of anonymity.

Momeni is also expected to meet Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif later Tuesday.

The Reuters news agency cited a senior Iranian official as saying that Tehran had received a proposal from mediators for a 10-day ceasefire in a bid to salvage the interim agreement that ultimately aimed at ending the war.

Skip next section WATCH: Strait of Hormuz: Iran's most powerful weapon?

WATCH: Strait of Hormuz: Iran's most powerful weapon?

Roughly a quarter of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas trade usually passes through the Strait of Hormuz. Has control of this strategic choke point become a more powerful weapon than a nuclear bomb?

Skip next section Bahrain says Iranian attack intercepted

Bahrain says Iranian attack intercepted

Bahrain's military said it intercepted Iranian aerial strikes on Tuesday in yet another attack on the Gulf kingdom.

"The air defences of the Bahrain Defence Force intercepted and destroyed a number of Iranian aerial aggressions," the military said in a statement.

Later, sirens sounded once again in the Gulf kingdom.

"The siren has been sounded, Citizens and residents are urged to remain calm and head to the nearest safe place," the Interior Ministry said in a statement on X.

The ministry advised residents to follow communications from official channels.

Skip next section WATCH: Iran engaged in 'full-scale war' with US, says Iran's President Pezeshkian

WATCH: Iran engaged in 'full-scale war' with US, says Iran's President Pezeshkian

Iran's president says the country is now engaged in a ‘full-scale war’ as fighting with the United States intensifies across the Middle East.

With US airstrikes entering a ninth consecutive night, Iranian attacks targeting American assets across the Gulf, and commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz largely disrupted, fears are growing of a wider regional conflict.

We speak to Middle East security expert Sara Bazoobandi about the risks of escalation and what's at stake for global trade and security.

Skip next section READ: Iran's oil supply threat extends beyond Strait of Hormuz

READ: Iran's oil supply threat extends beyond Strait of Hormuz

Oil and gas producers in the Gulf are seeking alternatives to the Strait of Hormuz as traffic again comes under fire. But is Iran also turning its attention to the pipelines meant to bypass the waterway?

Read more in depth DW coverage here.

Skip next section Jordan targeted by Iranian drones

Jordan targeted by Iranian drones

Jordan's military said it had intercepted five drones launched by Iran.

The state-run Petra news agency reported that the interceptions took place during overnight attacks

The agency cited a senior military source as confirming that the interceptions took place without any casualties or damage caused on the ground.

Iran's Revolutionary Guard said their forces targeted a complex housing US forces, confirming the attack.

Jordan hosts US forces and aircraft and has therefore been a frequent target of Iranian attacks since a preliminary deal between Washington and Tehran collapsed some two weeks ago.

Skip next section Lebanon's military deploys in southern town after Israeli withdrawal, sources say

Lebanon's military deploys in southern town after Israeli withdrawal, sources say

Lebanese forces had started deploying in the southern town of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh after Israel withdrew troops from the area, news agencies have reported, citing military sources.

The AFP news agency reported that Lebanese soldiers "have begun deploying to the village of Zawtar al-Gharbiyeh" in the Nabatieh region, after Israel occupied parts of southern Lebanon during their war with Hezbollah.

The move is in line with a plan sponsored by Washington that sees Lebanon's military assume greater responsibility for security and work to remove Iran-backed Hezbollah's weapons from the area.

According to the US-sponsored framework agreement between Lebanon and Israel, reached late June, Lebanon's armed forces will "gradually assume full and effective security responsibility in pilot zones, which will serve as the mechanism for phased and verified redeployments of the IDF (Israel Defense Forces) and the deployments of the LAF (Lebanese Armed Forces)."

Skip next section Iran's Revolutionary Guard claims attack on Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain

Iran's Revolutionary Guard claims attack on Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain

Iran's state media have reported that Iran's Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) claimed to have destroyed infrastructure belonging to US company Amazon in Bahrain.

IRNA reported that "multiple missiles" were launched against "Amazon's central data infrastructure in Bahrain, completely destroying it."

There has been no official confirmation from Bahrain and the claims cannot be independently verified.

Skip next section US military completes latest round of strikes on Iran

US military completes latest round of strikes on Iran

The US military said it completed another round of strikes against Iran, targeting military command centers, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, and air defense systems.

In a statement, US Central Command (CENTCOM) said the operation was aimed at degrading Iran's ability to continueattacks on commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's most important maritime trade routes.

CENTCOM said commercial shipping through the waterway remained ongoing despite the conflict. According to the US military, its forces have helped facilitate the transit of approximately 900 commercial vessels and 450 million barrels of crude oil through the strait since early May.

The US military also said its forces remain positioned to respond to what it described as Iranian aggression against civilian shipping, pledging to hold Tehran accountable for attacks on vessels navigating the strategic waterway.

entries.title: Jordan faces growing risk of becoming latest frontline state in US-Iran conflict
entries.summary: As Iranian attacks intensify, Jordan is emerging as the latest frontline state in the widening Middle East conflict. Why is the Hashemite Kingdom a target for Tehran?
entries.content:
July 21, 2026

As Iranian attacks intensify, Jordan is emerging as the latest frontline state in the widening Middle East conflict. Why is the Hashemite Kingdom a target for Tehran?

Air raid sirens again sounded in parts of Jordan early Tuesday as Iranian missiles targeted the kingdom.

"The air defense system intercepted and downed tonight three missiles coming from Iranian territory that entered Jordanian airspace and targeted the kingdom's territory," the army said in a statement.

No injuries were reported, but the latest strikes follow a series of attacks that have raised the stakes for one of the United States' closest Arab allies in the past days: Two US service members were killed and around 30 others injured by an Iranian missile strike on a US base in Jordan last week.

Jordan is one of the eight Arab countries that host permanent US military facilities. Some of the other host countries — particularly the Gulf states — have been the targets of Iranian attacks since the United States and Israel launched joint attacks on Iran in late February.

Though Iran launched missiles toward Jordan shortly thereafter, there has since been a lull in direct attacks on the kingdom, which has helped to intercept Iranian missiles launched toward Israel, arguing that it is acting in defense of its own airspace. 

"Iran has reserved itself the right to return fire on any state in the region that hosts US military assets or bases, and Jordan is one of them," Erwin van Veen, a senior research fellow at the Clingendael Institute in The Hague, told DW. "Only the options of Jordan's King Abdullah II are quite limited as Jordan is in a very precarious balance with respect to military and economic dependence on the Americans."

On Sunday, in a first diplomatic escalation, Jordan summoned Iran's top diplomat in Amman to demand an immediate end to what the foreign ministry called "unjustified and blatant Iranian attacks."

Jordan's political and economic tightrope walk

The US is Jordan's largest single foreign supporter. Under a seven-year Memorandum of Understanding signed in 2022, Washington agreed to provide $1.45 billion (€1.27bn) annually in economic and military assistance. In 2025, Congress increased this to $1.65 billion, which was triple the amount from 2010.

Furthermore, a Defense Cooperation Agreement signed in January 2021 grants US forces formalized access to Jordanian military facilities. According to a recent report, there are currently 4,000 US troops, more than in previous years, stationed in Jordan.

In March, however, Jordanian Foreign Minister Ayman Safadi insisted that there were no foreign military bases in the country. Kelly Petillo, program manager for Middle East and North Africa at the European Council on Foreign Relations, said one reason for downplaying foreign military presence in Jordan could be anti-US sentiment.

"Despite Jordan's efforts to remain neutral in this war, existing alliances such as the one with the US not only affect Jordan's fragile security but also cause public discontent," she told DW.

She speculated that Jordan, like its Gulf allies in the region, might be beginning to question whether its alliance with the US was beneficial to the country's stability or actually a cause of instability.

A 2026 poll by Arab Barometer, a nonpartisan public opinion research network, concluded that only 12% of Jordanians view US President Donald Trump's foreign policy favorably.

Jordan-Israel ties

"The other risk for Amman is that Iran's attacks could also place it in the same camp as Israel," Petillo warned. "This impression could cause further discontent among the wider public, creating space for protests as they were already seen during the war in Gaza, when people called for an end to the Israel-Jordan 1994 peace treaty."

In the past three years, relations between Israel and Jordan have become increasingly strained. In November 2023, the two countries recalled their ambassadors after Jordan's condemnation of Israel's conduct during the early days of its war in Gaza, which was unleashed after the Hamas attack on Israel on October 7, 2023. Hamas is considered a terrorist organization by the US, the EU and others.

Jordan has been at the forefront of efforts to push for a two-state solution, which would grant Palestinians their own state alongside Israel.

The Palestinian issue is of particular importance for Jordanian citizens as more than half of the population, including Queen Rania, has Palestinian roots. Around 2.4 million Palestinian refugees live in Jordan, according to the United Nations Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East (UNRWA).

But Jordan has repeatedly rejected any more displacement of Palestinians into the kingdom, particularly considering the high cost of hosting refugees whose return is anything but guaranteed.

"Jordan has been faced with a massive decline in USAID funding and the US pullback from UNRWA," Petillo said.

This comes as Jordan continues to struggle with high public debt and unemployment.

Popular anger related to Israel has recently been exacerbated by the expansion of settlements in the occupied West Bank, which the United Nations and rights groups say has accelerated since 2023. Increased settler violence has made life even worse for Palestinians in the West Bank.

According to the aid organization Save the Children, more than 1,000 Palestinian children were displaced by a surge in settler violence in the West Bank in the first six months of 2026. This was half the total number of children forced from their homes by settler violence over the entire previous three-year period.

"As the current Israeli coalition wrote in its own coalition agreement at the start of its tenure in 2022, it ultimately intends to annex the West Bank," Erwin van Veen said.

"Jordan is, in many ways, between a rock and a hard place, as it has little room for manoeuvre — neither toward Israel, Iran nor Washington," he added.

The West Bank, a landlocked territory of about 5,655 square kilometers (2,183 square miles), lies between Israel and Jordan. Israel took control of it from Jordan in the 1967 Six-Day War. According to the United Nations Security Council, "Israel's establishment of settlements in Palestinian territory occupied since 1967, including East Jerusalem, had no legal validity."

Edited by: Anne Thomas, Carla Bleiker

entries.title: Germany records rise in sexual violence against children
entries.summary: Germany's Criminal Police Office has reported over 17,000 cases of sexual violence against children in the past year. Organizations such as Zartbitter provide support to victims.
entries.content:
Germany

Germany records rise in sexual violence against children

July 21, 2026

Germany's Criminal Police Office has reported over 17,000 cases of sexual violence against children in the past year. Organizations such as Zartbitter provide support to victims.

Leonie Kirschstein knew that the number of sexual violence cases involving children and adolescents would not decline. The psychologist works for the registered nonprofit Zartbitter e.V., which has served as a support center for young victims in city of Cologne in western Germany for nearly four decades.  This pioneering organization counsels victims, conducts educational outreach in schools and even stages plays on the topic of sexual violence.

Kirschstein, who has worked for Zartbitter for three years, told DW that the number of unreported cases was much higher: "Crime statistics only include cases that have been reported to the police. But not the ones we deal with every day, such as cases involving peer violence. In many cases, these aren't reported at all, especially when the children are still very young. This group makes up the largest portion of the cases we handle in our counseling process."

Victims of sexual violence in every classroom

The issue of sexual violence is more topical than ever: The World Health Organization estimates that up to 1 million children and adolescents in Germany have experienced or are currently undergoing sexual violence at the hands of adults.

This means that, on average, in every German school classroom, there are one to two children who are victims of sexual violence. Kirschstein, who specializes in psychotherapy with children and adolescents, says: "We receive new cases every day; parents, in particular, call for an initial consultation. Unfortunately, I still very often find the victims wonder whether they themselves did something wrong — that is, whether they said 'no' explicitly and whether their refusal was understood as such by the perpetrator."

German Interior Minister Alexander Dobrindt wants to retain IP addresses

Leonie Kirschstein's observations align with the "Federal Assessment of Sexual Offenses Against Children and Adolescents 2025," which was released on July 21 in Berlin.

It states that the number of cases of sexual abuse of children and adolescents remains alarmingly high and has even risen in many areas. The number of sexual abuse offenses against minors under the age of 14 alone rose to 17,126 cases from 16,354 in 2024.

New record highs have also been reached for crimes involving "child pornography" and for the sexual abuse of children without physical contact.

German Interior Minister Alexander Dobrindt is sounding the alarm: "The high number of cases of sexual abuse of children and adolescents shows that we must protect our children even better — especially in the digital world," he said. "Perpetrators must not be allowed to feel safe and hide in the anonymity of the internet. In the fight against these repugnant crimes, our law enforcement agencies need effective tools such as IP address logging — to identify perpetrators, prosecute them consistently, and put an end to active abuse," Dobrindt told the media.

BKA President warns of 'cybergrooming'

To better prosecute cybercrime, the federal government is currently pushing for a law allowing the retention of IP addresses for three months. Dobrindt is confident that such a law could be passed as early as this fall. He has also called for the establishment of the post of European Commissioner for the Prevention of Sexual Abuse of Children and Adolescents.

Holger Münch, the president of the Federal Criminal Police Office (BKA), meanwhile, has warned against "cybergrooming." This refers to adults deliberately contacting children and adolescents online to initiate sexual contact.

"More than 98% of all perpetrators are male, with an average age of about 24; the victims are predominantly female and, on average, just under 12 years old. The primary risk factor is a lack of awareness of the risks associated with one's own online behavior. While teenage perpetrators tend to act out of boredom or just to pass the time, adult perpetrators often have sexual motives and specifically target minors," Münch said, adding that this often leads to a subsequent meeting and abuse.

Young people lack adequate legal protection

Independent Federal Commissioner Against Sexual Abuse of Children and Adolescents Kerstin Claus is calling for better protection, especially for teenagers in Germany

"Instances of 'sextortion' are increasing dramatically, and cybergrooming affects nearly one in three people between the ages of 14 and 17," she said. "And yet it is not currently categorized as a criminal offense."

What is not a criminal offense does not appear in statistics, and what is not counted there is not combated, Claus said. "That is why adolescents must be protected from cybergrooming, and sextortion must be classified as a distinct sexual offense. The federal situation report cannot even begin to reflect the true extent of the sexual violence to which young people are exposed online every day," she warned.

This article has been translated from German.

entries.title: Ousted Turkey opposition leader plans to create a new party
entries.summary: Turkey's main opposition CHP faces a potentially historic rupture after months of infighting over its leadership.
entries.content:
Turkey

Ousted Turkey opposition leader plans to create a new party

July 21, 2026

Turkey's main opposition CHP faces a potentially historic rupture after months of infighting over its leadership.

Turkey's ousted opposition leader, Ozgur Ozel, on Tuesday announced he would resign from the Republican People's Party, or CHP, and launch a new political party.

"Today, we are on the brink of a sad farewell that has become inevitable and whose time has come. However, we would like to share with the people that we carry the hope of a new beginning," Ozel said to more than 80 CHP lawmakers.

Ozel was removed as head of the CHP by court orderin May, in a ruling that also reinstated former leader Kemal Kilicdaroglu as interim leader.

Ozel and Kilicdaroglu disagreed on how to resolve the leadership issue permanently, leading to a dispute within the CHP.

New party did deepens split in Turkish opposition

Ozel's supporters say the case is part of a broader campaign by President Recep Tayyip Erdogan to weaken the opposition, while the government insists Turkey's courts operate independently.

Ozel is reportedly due to submit an application to the Interior Ministry to establish the new party, which, according to reports, will be called the "Yeni Party" ("New Party").

He enjoys strong support from CHP parliamentarians, and many of them are expected to defect from the CHP to join the new party once it is formed.

"Once the necessary procedures are completed, the new party we are about to establish will become the main opposition force in the Grand National Assembly by a wide margin," Ozel said. "We carry within us an unwavering hope for a new beginning," Ozel told lawmakers, who gave him a standing ovation.

Crackdown on the CHP

The dispute comes as investigations targeting CHP-run municipalities continue, including cases against Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu, a prominent rival to President Recep Tayyip Erdogan.

Imamoglu has been imprisoned since March last year and faces multiple criminal cases that could result in decades-long sentences.

After Imamoglu's arrest, Ozel played a key role in mobilizing Turkey's largest street protests in more than a decade, which saw the party's popularity rise in the polls.

Under Ozel's leadership, the CHP also made major gains in the 2024 municipal elections, strengthening its control of key cities including Istanbul and Ankara.

Edited by: Jenipher Camino Gonzalez

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Fact check: Trump wasn't edited out of Spain World Cup photo
entries.summary: After Spain won the FIFA World Cup, the Spanish Football Federation posted a photo of the trophy celebrations, raising questions about whether Donald Trump had been edited out. We took a closer look.
entries.content:
July 21, 2026

After Spain won the FIFA World Cup, the Spanish Football Federation posted a photo of the trophy celebrations, raising questions about whether Donald Trump had been edited out. We took a closer look.

Several posts have been circulating online claiming that US President Donald Trump was edited out of a photo postedby the Spanish Football Federation on its social media accounts.

Claim: One post on the social media platform X gained more than 11 million views while making the same claim.

"Lol Spain edited Trump out of the photo," the caption read.

DW Fact-check: False

The claim is false. The image is neither AI-generated nor altered. We found multiple pieces of evidence online debunking the claim.

There are several videos of Spain's trophy-lifting ceremony available online. In this video, Trump can be seen standing on the podium after handing the FIFA World Cup trophy to the victorious Spanish team, alongside FIFA President Gianni Infantino. Shortly afterwards, Infantino moves to the left while Trump moves towards the right side of the podium.

However, as soon as Infantino realizes that Trump is in the frame, he rushes over to him, seemingly telling him to give the players some space. Several photographers can be seen taking pictures in the foreground.

The photo in question was cropped slightly, which removed Trump and Infantino from the frame, perhaps to focus on the team. Trump was standing some distance away from the players and is therefore not visible in the image. But he was not edited out.

We found the same photo on Picture Alliance (and Newscom,) a photo agency that has distributed images throughout the championship.

Another picture from the same moment was published by the news agency Reuters, but from a different perspective. In this image, taken from a higher angle, Trump and Infantino can clearly be seen standing to the side of the winning team.

(Andreas Wisskirchen contributed to this piece.)

entries.title: DW correspondent Alican Uludag re-arrested in Turkey
entries.summary: The latest arrest comes two months after he was released from detention on charges including "insulting" President Recep Tayyip Erdogan.
entries.content:
July 21, 2026

The latest arrest comes two months after he was released from detention on charges including "insulting" President Recep Tayyip Erdogan.

Deutsche Welle correspondent Alican Uludag has been re-arrested in Turkey on Tuesday, just two months after being granted conditional release.

Uludag announced on social media, "I'm being taken into custody," and Deutsche Welle confirmed his arrest.

"The re-arrest of our colleague Alican Uludag is deeply concerning. Journalistic work must not be criminalized," DW Director General Barbara Massing said, adding, "we expect his rights to be fully respected and demand a fair trial in accordance with the rule of law."

He was taken into custody over an investigation into a post on X criticizing the Turkish judiciary, according to the Ankara Chief Public Prosecutor's Office.

Detained over social media posts

Uludag was first detained in February, accused of insulting President Recep Tayyip Erdogan, spreading misleading information and disparaging state institutions or the Turkish state in 22 social media posts.

"Insulting" or disparaging Erdogan is a criminal offense in Turkey.

He remained in pretrial detention until May 21, when a court granted him conditional release.

Uludag denied all the charges, saying he was objectively reporting.

Uludag draws support

Uludag has, in Turkey's restrictive media landscape, continued to focus on contentious issues such as the judicial system, human rights violations and corruption.

He has "independently researched and reported on the judiciary, politics and society in Turkey in the public interest," Massing said.

During his previous detention, numerous national and international organizations, as well as political representatives, called for his release.

After his initial release, Reporters Without Borders said, "he should not have spent even a single day in detention because of either his reporting or his commentary."

Deutsche Welle said it remained in touch with his lawyers following the latest developments.

Edited by: Jenipher Camino Gonzalez
